perturbation.original_text: This Affiliate Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.changed_text: This Affiliate Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to Nevada introduces potential conflicts. While both states have securities laws, their interpretation and enforcement may differ significantly. More specifically, this contract concerns the sale and transfer of stock and the original text indicates Delaware law, to remove the application of the Delaware law would increase the legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law, specifically regarding fiduciary duties and shareholder rights in mergers and acquisitions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, particularly 8 Del. C. § 141, outlines the fiduciary duties of corporate directors, which are especially relevant during mergers. By changing the governing law to Nevada, the contract attempts to sidestep these established Delaware precedents, potentially weakening shareholder protections in the event of disputes related to the merger. Nevada law has different standards that may provide less protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The modification changes the governing law from Delaware to Nevada, potentially sidestepping Delaware's established legal precedents, specifically 8 Del. C. § 141, which outlines the fiduciary duties of corporate directors. By changing the governing law, the contract attempts to weaken shareholder protections. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions "every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors". Changing the governing law can be viewed as an Omission Legal Contradiction because removing the application of the Delaware law would increase the legal risk.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned understands that PSS is under no ------------------------------------------------ obligation to file a registration statement with the SEC covering the --------------------------------------------------------------------- disposition of the undersigned's shares of PSS Common ----------------------------------------------------- Stock or to take any other action necessary to make compliance with an ---------------------------------------------------------------------- exemption from such registration available.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned understands that PSS is under no obligation to file a registration statement with the SEC covering the disposition of the undersigned's shares of PSS Common Stock.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or to take any other action necessary to make compliance with an exemption from such registration available,' the contract suggests that PSS has no responsibility to facilitate the affiliate's compliance with securities laws, even if an exemption exists. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing that exists in most contracts and could be argued as hindering the affiliate's ability to legally sell their shares.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Act of 1933, specifically Section 5 regarding the registration of securities.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S. Code § 77e) generally requires the registration of securities before their sale. While exemptions exist, removing the clause about PSS taking 'any other action necessary to make compliance with an exemption from such registration available' could be interpreted as PSS hindering the affiliate's ability to utilize those exemptions, potentially violating the spirit of the Act by making it unreasonably difficult to sell the shares legally.

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the clause obligating PSS to 'take any other action necessary to make compliance with an exemption from such registration available.' This omission directly impacts the affiliate's ability to utilize exemptions under the Securities Act of 1933, as described in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly state this obligation, the omission hinders compliance with securities laws, making it unreasonably difficult to sell shares legally. This undermines the overall regulatory framework established by the Securities Act, creating a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: PSS agrees, for a period of three years ------------------------ after the effective date of the Merger, to file on a timely basis all reports required to be filed by it pursuant to Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended, so that the public information provisions of Rule 145(d) promulgated by the SEC as the same are presently in effect will be available to the undersigned in the event the undersigned desires to transfer any shares of PSS Common Stock issued to the undersigned pursuant to the Merger.
perturbation.changed_text: PSS agrees to file reports pursuant to Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the time constraint of 'for a period of three years after the effective date of the Merger' and the clause 'so that the public information provisions of Rule 145(d) promulgated by the SEC as the same are presently in effect will be available to the undersigned in the event the undersigned desires to transfer any shares of PSS Common Stock issued to the undersigned pursuant to the Merger' creates ambiguity. Public companies are typically expected to file regular reports, but this omission removes the assurance for the specified shareholders.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 13(a) regarding periodic reporting requirements for publicly traded companies.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. § 78m(a)) mandates that publicly traded companies file periodic reports with the SEC. While PSS would likely be obligated to file these reports regardless, removing the clause that ensures these reports would enable compliance with Rule 145(d) potentially disadvantages the affiliate shareholder. Rule 145(d) provides a safe harbor for the resale of securities acquired in certain business combinations, but its availability depends on the issuer meeting its reporting obligations. Thus, this omission introduces legal risk by obscuring the affiliate's ability to reliably utilize Rule 145(d).

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Securities Exchange Act of 1934 The Securities and Exchange Act of 1934 ("1934 Act," or "Exchange Act") primarily regulates transactions of securities in the secondary market . As such, the 1934 Act typically governs transactions which take place between parties which are not the original issuer , such as trades that retail investors execute through brokerage companies. In contrast, the Securities Act of 1933 prior to the Exchange Act established regulations for issuers and listings on the primary market . Disclosures To protect investors, Congress crafted a mandatory disclosure process designed to force companies to disclose information that investors would find pertinent to making investment decisions. In addition, the Exchange Act regulates the exchanges on which securities are sold. Regulation FD is the primary section of the Exchange Act which discusses disclosures. Reporting Requirements The required disclosures and forms of disclosure vary depending on the situation and the registrant. In general, under Section 13(a) of the Exchange Act (codified in 15 U.S.C. § 78m ), companies with registered publicly held securities and companies of a certain size are called "reporting companies," meaning that they must make periodic disclosures by filing annual reports (called a Form 10-K ) and quarterly reports (called a Form 10-Q ). Reporting companies must also promptly disclose certain important events (called a Form 8-K ). These periodic reports include or incorporate by reference types of information that would help investors decide whether a company's security is a good investment. Information in these reports includes information about the company's officers and directors, the company's line of business, audited financial statements , and the management discussion and analysis section. The Exchange Act also mandates disclosure at certain crucial points so that investors can make an informed decision before purchasing stock. Sections 14(a)-(c) (codified in 15 U.S.C. § 78n(a)-(c) ) govern disclosure during proxy contests, when various parties might solicit an investor's vote on a corporate action, or to vote for certain board members. All disclosure materials must be filed with the SEC. Tender Offers The Securities Exchange Act requires disclosure of important information by anyone seeking to acquire more than 5 percent of a company's securities by direct purchase or tender offer . Such an offer often is extended in an effort to gain control of the company. If a party makes a tender offer, the Williams Act governs (codified as 15 U.S.C. § 78m(d)-(e) ). A tender offeror must also file disclosure documents with the SEC that disclose its future plans relating to its holdings in the company. This information allows investors to decide whether to sell or not. Securities and Exchange Commission Section 4 of the Exchange Act established the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) , which is the federal agency responsible for enforcing securities laws. SEC Required Disclosures One important function of the SEC is to ensure that
perturbation.justification: The omission of the three-year time constraint and the clause regarding Rule 145(d) creates a legal contradiction. While Section 13(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as stated in both the Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 2, mandates periodic reports for publicly held securities, the original contract text provided a specific assurance to the undersigned shareholders related to Rule 145(d). By removing this assurance, the modified text weakens the ability of shareholders to reliably utilize Rule 145(d). Although PSS is still obligated to file reports as per 15 U.S.C. § 78m(a) according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, the original provision ensuring the report's availability for Rule 145(d) compliance is omitted, creating uncertainty and potentially impacting the shareholders' ability to transfer shares, which falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.
